hero.desc: I design intuitive digital products focusing on user needs, visual craft, and systems thinking — from initial sketch to launch.

items.title: UI/UX Design
items.desc: Designs focused on usability, visual hierarchy, and interaction craft; from wireframes to pixel-perfect interfaces.

items.title: Design Systems
items.desc: Scalable token architectures, component libraries, and design-to-code documentation enabling teams to work in harmony.

items.title: User Research
items.desc: Interviews, usability testing, card sorting, and synthesis frameworks to ground design decisions in real user behavior.

contact.title: Let's work
contact.body: Have a product challenge or design role in mind? I'm always open to discussing new opportunities and ideas.

about.lede: I am a Product Designer creating user-centered digital products since 2018. I independently designed the UI/UX of the RouteWise App, contributed to the design team of Akaunting, and delivered design solutions for the Ticaret Bakanlığı Free Zones Platform, TUSAŞ Hub, ASELSAN Hub, and EntekBir Mobile projects. All are live and in active use — built around usability, accessibility, and craft. Beyond UI/UX, I work in motion, branding, and graphic design.
about.ctaLede: Have a product challenge or role in mind? I'm always open to discussing new opportunities.

hireModal.lede: Tell me about your project or role and I'll get back within 6 hours.
hireModal.descriptionPlaceholder: A few sentences about the product, goals, and what kind of design help you're looking for…

items.title: StandardFlow Website UI Design
items.desc: End-to-end website UI design — landing page, marketing flow, and conversion-focused interactions for a SaaS product. Full case study covering visual system and key screens.
highlights: Full landing-page flow built on a tight visual system
Hero, features, social proof and pricing sections — conversion-focused layout
Reusable patterns and components, ready for handoff
Built in Figma with attention to typography and motion craft
galleryCaptions: Join as a Founder — founder signup path
Join as an Investor — investor signup path

caseStudy.problem: StandardFlow needed a complete marketing presence — landing, feature deep-dives, pricing, and supporting surfaces — all bound by one cohesive visual system that read as a single product rather than three different sites.

process.heading: Setting the visual ground
process.body: Started with the foundations — a type system anchored on Archivo for display weight and a calmer neutral for body, a single dominant accent against deep neutrals, and a tight spacing scale that could survive both hero density and pricing-table density without breaking.

process.heading: Composing the marketing arc
process.body: Designed the landing as a narrative sequence: a quiet hero with one focal CTA, then social proof, feature blocks paced for breathing room, pricing as the decisive moment, and a closing CTA. The page is meant to read as a single argument, not a stack of sections.

process.heading: Lifting into reusable patterns
process.body: Once the landing felt right, the hero structure, feature blocks, and pricing tiles were extracted as reusable patterns — so the rest of the marketing surfaces could ship without re-inventing the visual language.

decisions.title: One accent, no second voice
decisions.rationale: The temptation in B2B SaaS marketing is to spice things up with secondary colors. I kept a single dominant accent against neutrals — confidence over energy. Restraint became the system's identity.

decisions.title: Hierarchy via weight, not just size
decisions.rationale: Hero display and supporting copy live closer in size than typical; weight and density carry the hierarchy. Reads sharper on mid-screens and keeps the page calmer overall.

decisions.title: Marketing gutters > product gutters
decisions.rationale: Wider section padding than I'd use inside a product UI. Each block lands before the next begins — especially needed on tall scroll surfaces where pacing matters as much as content.

outcome.narrative: The client team described the result as 'one product, one voice' — exactly the read I was aiming for. The visual system now serves as the ground for future product surfaces, with patterns ready to extend without renegotiating the core decisions.

learnings: Tight type scale + generous spacing reads calmer than the opposite — useful for B2B SaaS where trust beats spectacle.
Designing the marketing flow as a single arc (not a sequence of independent pages) let me pace visual energy across the whole journey.
A single accent forces every decision through the same color filter — harder upfront, but produces a noticeably tighter system.

walkthrough.title: Walkthrough
walkthrough.intro: A closer look at the onboarding entry — the moment a new user decides how to use the product. Each marker explains one decision behind the surface.

hotspots.title: Brand mark, not application chrome
hotspots.body: The logotype sits centered above the headline rather than tucked into a corner. Onboarding is the moment of first encounter — the brand earns the largest single piece of real estate above the fold, not the navigation.

hotspots.title: Gradient on display, not on chrome
hotspots.body: The 'Join StandardFlow' headline is the only element with a subtle silver gradient on the page. Gradients applied to buttons or boxes feel like decoration; applied to display type they read as intentional material. One place to use it, and it's here.

hotspots.title: Equal weight — and that's the point
hotspots.body: On a pricing page I'd make one card visually inevitable. Here the opposite is true: a founder and an investor are equally valid users, neither is a 'recommended' choice. Equal weight communicates that this is a fork, not a funnel.

hotspots.title: Monochrome icons — category, not status
hotspots.body: Icons stay neutral white-on-dark. Adding color would imply hierarchy or status (active/recommended). Since both choices are equally valid, icons function purely as category markers, not signals.

hotspots.title: Link-style CTA, full-card hit area
hotspots.body: The 'Sign up as Investor →' affordance reads as a link rather than a button. The reason: the entire card is clickable. A button would have visually contradicted that — the link + arrow tells the user where they're going while letting them tap anywhere.

hotspots.title: Secondary path, secondary weight
hotspots.body: Existing users live at the bottom with the lightest type weight on the page. New users are the primary path here, so their decision occupies the visual center; returning users get the minimum surface area they need to find login.

items.title: Accounting Software UI Design
items.desc: Interface design and design-system work for an accounting SaaS — invoices, reports, and dashboard surfaces with a scalable component library underneath.
highlights: Interface design for invoices, reports, and dashboard surfaces
Component-library foundation supporting consistent product UI
Co-designed with a partner designer on layout and data patterns
Built in Figma; legacy XD/Illustrator artwork integrated where useful

caseStudy.problem: The client had a working accounting SaaS, but years of feature additions had left the interface fragmented — inconsistent components, no shared visual language, and a codebase that made every new screen a negotiation. They needed a full redesign of the core surfaces and a design system underneath that engineering could actually build from.

process.heading: Auditing the existing interface
process.body: Before designing anything new, we mapped the existing surface area — every screen variant, every inconsistency. The audit revealed that the visual drift wasn't random; it traced back to a missing token layer. There was no shared source for color, spacing, or type, so each screen had made its own decisions over time.

process.heading: Token layer before screens
process.body: We established the foundation first: color roles (not raw hex values), a spacing scale, type ramp, and radius tokens. With both designers writing to the same token set, every downstream decision compounded — we didn't need to re-agree on what 'medium padding' meant each time.

process.heading: Surface ownership with a shared foundation
process.body: With the system grounded, we split surface ownership. One designer led invoices and reports; the other took dashboards and data tables. Daily syncs caught divergence before it hardened. The shared token layer kept both tracks looking like one product even when designed in parallel.

decisions.title: Foundation before features
decisions.rationale: We could have started with the most visible screens to show client progress fast. Instead we locked down the token layer first. Felt slow for the first two weeks, but eliminated rework — every screen decision traced back to the same source.

decisions.title: Data density as a design constraint
decisions.rationale: Accounting UIs are inherently dense — invoices, line items, totals, dates. Rather than fight the density, we leaned into generous row height and tight typographic contrast so tables were scannable without being sparse. Sparse felt wrong for the domain.

decisions.title: Shared tokens, split surfaces
decisions.rationale: In a two-designer setup, the risk is subtle drift — same intent, different execution. Explicit surface ownership (who owns what) combined with a shared token set was the arrangement that prevented the classic 'designed it differently' problem without requiring constant coordination.

outcome.narrative: The redesign and component library shipped together — engineering took the Figma hand-off and built directly from the design system. All core surfaces (invoices, reports, dashboard) were covered in a single delivery. The client's team had a foundation they could extend without renegotiating the base decisions each time.

learnings: Token layer first felt slow but proved to be the only path — every screen decision compounded on a shared foundation instead of diverging.
Two designers on one product is a coordination problem as much as a design problem. Surface ownership + shared tokens was the structure that made it work.
Dense data handled well is its own form of craft — accounting interfaces deserve as much design attention as landing pages.

walkthrough.title: Walkthrough
walkthrough.intro: A closer look at the color token system that anchored the product. Each marker explains one structural decision — not the swatches themselves, but the rules that made them a system.
walkthrough.alt: Akaunting design system — color token documentation

hotspots.title: Brand Colors — single dominant voice
hotspots.body: The brand green sits at the top of the system, alone in its row. There's no 'second brand color' — accents, state signals, and neutrals do the rest of the work. One dominant voice prevents the rainbow drift that breaks accounting UIs over time.

hotspots.title: Three controlled variants per brand color
hotspots.body: Darker, Lighter, Subtle — each brand color ships with three pre-decided variants rather than letting designers pick tints per-screen. Eliminates the 'similar but slightly different' shades that accumulate without a token system.

hotspots.title: Contrast Colors — second axis, same structure
hotspots.body: The contrast color (used when green isn't appropriate) follows the exact same Darker/Lighter/Subtle structure. The token system is a vocabulary, and every word in it conjugates the same way.

hotspots.title: State Colors — status, never decoration
hotspots.body: Error, Warning, Info, Success live in their own row. Status communication never borrows the brand color, and the brand color never borrows status meaning. The two axes stay independent.

hotspots.title: Text Colors as named tokens
hotspots.body: Four text weights — not picked grays. Designers reach for Text 1/2/3/4 instead of #595959 vs #6E6E6E. Removes one of the most common sources of inconsistency in a multi-person product team.

hotspots.title: Hex values inline — handoff built in
hotspots.body: Every swatch carries its hex value beside the label. The documentation is the source for both designers and engineers — no separate spec doc to drift out of sync. The system is the handoff.

items.title: Project Management App
items.desc: Product design for a project management tool — landing surface and key in-app screens, focused on clarity for cross-functional teams.
highlights: Marketing landing page paired with in-app product screens
Information hierarchy tuned for fast scanning across long task lists
Designed in Figma with consistent token and component usage

caseStudy.problem: The client came with a familiar complaint: every project management tool they'd tried — Jira, Asana, ClickUp — felt heavier than the work it was supposed to organize. They wanted an alternative that earned its space on the screen: simpler defaults, clearer hierarchy, and a marketing page that promised the same thing the product delivered.

process.heading: Mapping where complexity actually lives
process.body: Before designing screens, I traced where existing tools lose users — not in the feature count, but in the moment-to-moment scanning of long task lists. Status, priority, assignee, due date, project tag, comment count — every row carries six pieces of information competing for attention. That's the real problem to solve.

process.heading: Designing for scannability first
process.body: Built the task list around a clear primary axis: task title gets the typographic weight, everything else recedes into a calm secondary layer. Status uses color as hierarchy (not decoration), so a glance down the list reveals what needs attention without reading every row. Density tuned to fit the list on screen without packing it.

process.heading: Landing as a product extension, not a separate site
process.body: The marketing page was designed with the same components, type ramp, and spacing as the in-app surfaces. When a visitor clicks through to the product, there's no visual rupture — the promise on the landing page is literally the same surface they're about to use.

decisions.title: Breathable over packed
decisions.rationale: PM tools default to packing as much information per row as possible. I went the other way — generous row height, calm dividers, content given room to breathe. Loses a few rows per viewport, gains massively in scannability. The whole point was 'lighter than the work.'

decisions.title: Status as color hierarchy, not decoration
decisions.rationale: Color in PM tools is usually applied per-tag, ending up rainbow-ish and noisy. I used color exclusively for status — overdue, in progress, done — so the color layer becomes a meaningful signal rather than visual decoration.

decisions.title: Single visual system across landing and app
decisions.rationale: Many products have a 'marketing brand' and a 'product brand' that read as two different companies. Designing both surfaces from the same component set made the transition from visitor to user feel like continuation, not arrival in a new place.

outcome.narrative: The product shipped with a marketing surface that visitors described as 'finally calm' compared to incumbents. The task list pattern — title-first, status-as-color, breathable density — became the anchor pattern that future product screens extended from without renegotiating the base.

learnings: PM tool complaints aren't really about feature count — they're about scanning load. Solving the right problem started with naming it correctly.
Using color as hierarchy (one job, one signal) instead of decoration is one of the highest-leverage moves in dense UI.
Treating landing and product as one surface — not two — eliminates a category of brand-coherence problems before they appear.

walkthrough.title: Walkthrough
walkthrough.intro: A closer look at the Kanban board — the screen most users live in. Each marker explains one decision behind keeping a dense task surface scannable rather than packed.
walkthrough.alt: Project Management App — Kanban board view

hotspots.title: Current project, loud
hotspots.body: Project 2 is highlighted; Project 1 dims. Most PM tools leave 'where you are' implicit. Making it visually loud lets users return from a deep tab without spending a second re-orienting.

hotspots.title: Views as peers, not modes
hotspots.body: Kanban is active but List, Timeline, Calendar sit beside it as siblings. 'How do I look at my work right now' is the first decision the user makes — it shouldn't be hidden in a deeper menu.

hotspots.title: Status dot mirrors column state
hotspots.body: Each column header carries a color dot tied to that state, and the same color reappears inside the column's task cards. One color = one meaning across the whole surface. Color never doubles as decoration.

hotspots.title: Count badges — workload at a glance
hotspots.body: 'Open 3, To-Do 1, Doing 4, Done 0, Close 3' — the count next to each header lets an operator scan workload by column without counting cards. Volume signal without visual noise.

hotspots.title: Task card — three-line hierarchy
hotspots.body: Title at primary weight, date as supporting metadata, avatars and status icons as accent. Three pieces of information per card, fixed hierarchy. The card stays breathable instead of packing six values per row.

hotspots.title: Empty state — invitation, not absence
hotspots.body: Done column is empty, but instead of a blank space the user sees a dashed 'Add Task +' affordance. Maintains visual rhythm across columns and invites action rather than just signaling absence.

items.title: Pricing Page UI Design
items.desc: A focused pricing-page exploration — clear plan comparison, conversion-oriented hierarchy, and a small interaction system around toggles and CTAs.
highlights: Plan comparison tuned for fast scanning
Toggle interactions and supporting micro-states
Conversion-led visual hierarchy on primary CTA

caseStudy.problem: The existing pricing page wasn't converting. Visitors were landing, scanning the plan grid, and leaving without choosing. The plans themselves were fine — the page wasn't doing its job: helping a visitor decide. The brief was specific: keep the plans, fix the decision.

process.heading: Reading the original page as a decision tool
process.body: Audited the existing page not as a layout but as a decision flow. Three plans presented with equal visual weight, dense feature lists below each, a toggle for monthly/yearly. Every plan asked for the same level of attention — which meant none of them got it. The page was a comparison grid, not a recommendation.

process.heading: Picking the recommended plan and committing to it
process.body: The single biggest decision was visual: which plan should the page steer toward? Once the recommended plan was chosen with the client, the entire layout reorganized around making that plan visually inevitable — slightly taller, accent border, badge above the title, primary CTA color reserved only for it. The other two plans became context, not competition.

process.heading: Compressing the feature comparison
process.body: Long per-plan feature lists were collapsed into a single comparison table below the main cards. The cards above show only the 3-4 features that actually differ between plans; the table is there for visitors who want to verify, not the first decision moment. Decision first, verification second.

decisions.title: One plan visually inevitable
decisions.rationale: Equal visual weight across plans is technically 'fair' but practically paralyzing. By making the recommended plan visibly the answer — height, accent, badge, sole primary CTA color — the page started doing the decision work for the visitor instead of asking them to do it.

decisions.title: Card content stripped to differentiators
decisions.rationale: Each plan card showed only the 3-4 features that actually differed between tiers, not the full feature list. The 'everything else is the same' part lives in the comparison table below — accessible but not in the way during the decision moment.

decisions.title: Toggle as price-clarity, not delight
decisions.rationale: Monthly/yearly toggle was kept deliberately understated — no animation flourish, just a clean state change and an explicit 'save 20%' anchored to the yearly position. The toggle's job is to remove a price question, not to be a moment.

outcome.narrative: The redesign reframed the page from comparison-grid to decision-tool. Post-launch, the recommended plan saw a meaningful lift in selection — exactly the outcome the brief asked for. The patterns from this study (recommended plan emphasis, differentiator-only cards, restrained toggle) carried into adjacent product pages later.

learnings: Equal visual weight across options is a default that quietly transfers decision burden to the visitor. Picking a recommendation and committing to it visually is doing real work, not playing favorites.
Pricing pages have two jobs — decision and verification — and they need different design treatments. Cards for decision, table for verification.
Restraint on toggles and micro-interactions reads as confidence on pricing pages. Decoration here signals salesmanship; calm signals trust.

walkthrough.title: Walkthrough
walkthrough.intro: A closer look at the pricing page — the screen where a visitor either converts or leaves. Each marker explains one decision behind turning a comparison grid into a decision tool.

hotspots.title: Page framed as a decision
hotspots.body: 'Pick a plan that is right for you' — the headline names the user's task, not the product's features. The whole page is shaped around helping one decision happen, not selling the catalog.

hotspots.title: Toggle — pricing clarity, not delight
hotspots.body: The monthly/yearly toggle stays deliberately understated — no animation flourish, just a clean state change with an explicit '2 months free' anchored to yearly. The toggle's job is to remove a price question, not to be a moment.

hotspots.title: Recommended plan, visually inevitable
hotspots.body: Premium Cloud is the answer — yellow card surface, green 'PREMIUM CLOUD' label, dollar value at maximum weight. While other cards stay neutral, this one pulls the eye. The page makes the decision for the visitor instead of asking them to do it.

hotspots.title: Cards — differentiators only
hotspots.body: Each card lists only the features that actually differ between plans (Unlimited Users, Companies, Bank Feeds, Ticket Support). The full feature list lives below in the comparison table. Decision first, verification second.

hotspots.title: Reassurance answers the limit question
hotspots.body: Three large 'Unlimited' callouts sit between the cards and the comparison table — addressing the unspoken question 'but how many can I actually have' before it becomes a click-blocker. Anticipating the friction is part of the design.

hotspots.title: Comparison table — for verification, not decision
hotspots.body: The exhaustive feature comparison lives below the cards, intentionally. Visitors who've already chosen come here to verify; visitors who haven't decided weren't going to find the answer in a 30-row matrix. Two jobs, two surfaces.

items.title: Routewise UI Design
items.desc: This UI design focuses on making multi-vehicle tracking intuitive and effortless. It transforms complex data into a clean, user-friendly experience.
highlights: Restrained visual language for a high-trust category
Strong typographic hierarchy across hero and service sections
Designed in Figma with reusable section patterns

caseStudy.problem: Routewise was a brand-new fleet-tracking SaaS being built from the ground up. There was no existing UI to inherit and no design history to extend — just a domain (multi-vehicle tracking) that's inherently dense: live positions, status per vehicle, alerts, history, drivers, routes. The brief was to ship an MVP that read as trustworthy and modern, not as another technical-looking fleet tool.

process.heading: Naming what 'calm' means in a dense product
process.body: Fleet-tracking UIs default to information overload — every pixel earning its keep with data. The first decision was philosophical: we'd treat calm as a feature, not a luxury. The dashboard would carry the same information density as competitors, but it would feel quieter. The 'how' followed from that 'what'.

process.heading: Building the visual ground
process.body: Set up the foundation with restrained color (status colors reserved, never decorative), generous spacing on the dashboard grid (each card given room to land), and a single accent that doesn't compete with status signals. The map and table surfaces share the same component vocabulary so switching between them feels like one product, not two views.

process.heading: Designing the dashboard as a glanceable surface
process.body: The dashboard's job isn't to show everything — it's to surface what needs attention. Critical alerts get a primary visual lane; everything else lives in calm secondary blocks. An operator scanning the screen at 8am should know in two seconds whether the fleet is fine or whether something needs them. That shape drove every subsequent layout decision.

decisions.title: Calm as a feature, not a luxury
decisions.rationale: Every fleet-tracking competitor screams data. Choosing calm was a positioning decision as much as a design decision — it signals 'we don't need to overwhelm you to be powerful.' Risk: looking less capable at first glance. Tradeoff worth it for the kind of users we wanted.

decisions.title: Status colors reserved, never decorative
decisions.rationale: Color was kept exclusively for vehicle/route status (active, idle, alert). Categorical tags, navigation, and chrome stay neutral. This makes the color layer informational, so a glance at the screen actually tells you something rather than just looking colorful.

decisions.title: Map and table share one component vocabulary
decisions.rationale: Many fleet products treat the map view and the table view as separate UIs with different visual styles. We built them from the same component set — the status pill in the table looks the same on a map pin tooltip — so the user's mental model carries cleanly across views.

outcome.narrative: The MVP shipped with marketing surfaces and product screens reading as a single product. The visual language — restrained color, generous spacing, status-as-signal — became the anchor for subsequent feature work without each new screen renegotiating the base decisions. The 'calm fleet tool' positioning gave the product a distinct read among loud competitors.

learnings: Density and calm aren't opposites. A dense product can still feel quiet if color and spacing are doing meaningful work, not decorative work.
In data-heavy products, the dashboard's most important job is triage — surfacing what needs attention. That's a design problem, not a data problem.
Shipping marketing and product from a single component set early eliminates a category of late-stage brand-coherence problems.

walkthrough.title: Walkthrough
walkthrough.intro: A closer look at the fleet control panel — the screen an operator opens first every morning. Each marker explains one decision about how density and calm coexist on the same surface.

hotspots.title: Favorites above Dashboards
hotspots.body: The sidebar splits 'Favorites / Recently' from the full Dashboards list. Daily operators look at the same two or three things every morning — surfacing those above the full nav saves a cognitive hop and respects the actual usage pattern.

hotspots.title: KPI cards — three signals in one glance
hotspots.body: Each KPI pairs a current value, a circular progress ring (against target), and a trend delta below. Three pieces of information in one card means the morning check is genuinely one glance, not four.

hotspots.title: Status as color, not decoration
hotspots.body: The green +7.4% is the only color in this row aside from the chart fills. Trend direction is the one piece of information that demanded instant visual confirmation — color is reserved for that, never spent on decoration.

hotspots.title: Map and Stats — equal-weight pair
hotspots.body: Map and stats sit side-by-side on the same row, equal width. Fleet operators think both geographically and numerically — neither is the 'primary' view, so the layout treats them as siblings rather than nesting one inside the other.

hotspots.title: Card chrome — learned once, used everywhere
hotspots.body: Every card uses the same header: icon, label, optional edit/menu affordance, white surface. Once a user learns one card, they've learned all of them. The chrome stays out of the way of the data.

hotspots.title: Bottom row — fleet at a glance
hotspots.body: Battery, fuel, road, device status — four metrics that map to the operational health of the fleet. The dashboard's job is triage; these four are the morning check before any deeper navigation.

items.title: LogoType Design
items.desc: A collection of logotype and brand-identity explorations — wordmarks, lockups, and typographic studies across multiple brand contexts.
highlights: Wordmarks and lockups across multiple brand directions
Typographic exploration and custom letterform tuning
Crafted in Illustrator with supporting Photoshop work

caseStudy.problem: A logotype designed for a specific brand engagement — not a generic identity exercise. The brief asked for a mark that read as serious without leaning on the default toolbox of contemporary branding: no decorative icon, no over-styled gradient, no trend signal. The challenge was to make restraint feel intentional rather than empty.

process.heading: Choosing restraint over symbol
process.body: The instinct in 2021 branding was to add a symbol — a clever mark, a stylized monogram, something for a favicon. I went the other way: the wordmark alone, no icon, no flourish. If the letterforms couldn't carry the brand, no symbol would rescue them. That choice set the scope of the rest of the work.

process.heading: Treating typography as the entire identity
process.body: With no symbol to hide behind, every typographic detail had to earn its place. Spent the bulk of the time inside the letterforms themselves — adjusting terminals, tuning x-height for optical balance, hand-correcting kerning pair-by-pair. The whole 'design' lived at the scale of millimeters across the mark.

process.heading: Designing lockups as a small system
process.body: Even a single wordmark needs to behave across contexts — horizontal, stacked, single-color, on dark, in tiny favicon sizes. Designed the lockup variants as a small system with shared proportions, so the mark stayed recognisably itself regardless of where it appeared.

decisions.title: No symbol, no decoration
decisions.rationale: Adding a symbol is the default move and the easiest way to make a logo feel 'complete.' Resisting it forced the wordmark to actually do the work. Easier to look generic this way; harder to look generic when the typography is genuinely tuned.

decisions.title: Detail at millimeter scale
decisions.rationale: Kerning, terminal shape, x-height ratio, optical balance — these usually live in the background of identity work. Here they were the work. Most viewers won't consciously notice them; the difference between a tuned logotype and an untuned one is felt, not seen.

decisions.title: Lockups as a small system
decisions.rationale: A logotype that only works at one size and one orientation isn't an identity, it's a sketch. Designing lockup variants from the start ensured the mark could survive the contexts it would actually live in — including the unkind ones (small favicons, single-color print).

outcome.narrative: The final mark relies entirely on its letterforms — no symbol, no flourish, no trend signal. It reads as quietly confident across the contexts it was designed for, and the lockup system gives it room to extend without losing the core proportions. Restraint, properly executed, became the identity.

learnings: Skipping the symbol is a constraint that forces typography to be genuinely good. It's a discipline more than a style.
Most of identity work happens at scales no one consciously sees — but the cumulative effect is felt immediately.
Designing the lockup variants alongside the primary mark — not afterwards — prevents the 'works in the case study, fails in real use' problem.

walkthrough.title: Walkthrough
walkthrough.intro: A closer look at the logotype in three contexts. Each marker explains one decision behind keeping the identity in the letterforms rather than reaching for a symbol.

hotspots.title: Primary lockup — wordmark + tagline
hotspots.body: The full wordmark sits with the tagline 'Progress with data & automation' beneath it at a much lighter weight. Tagline supports, never competes — sized and weighted to recede the moment the wordmark is read.

hotspots.title: Wordmark only — the same letterforms
hotspots.body: Stripped to the wordmark alone, the identity still holds. The letterforms — kerning, terminal shape, the dot detail on 'o' — were tuned so that the wordmark could stand without a tagline and still read as the brand.

hotspots.title: App icon — letterforms become the mark
hotspots.body: Even the smallest format uses letterforms — 'C' and 'o' lifted from the wordmark and reframed as a circular composition. No separate icon was designed; the wordmark contains its own mark, just rearranged for tiny contexts.

hotspots.title: Three contexts, one identity
hotspots.body: Light blue, peach, near-black — three different surface contexts. The logo holds together because proportions and letterforms don't change with the background; only the tonal layer adapts. Identity stays constant, context changes around it.

items.title: Pace App UI/UX Design
items.desc: A daily-spending budgeting app for iOS — designed and built end-to-end. Instead of a monthly report you read too late, Pace answers one question: how much can I spend today and stay on track?
highlights: Budget reframed as a daily pace — not a monthly post-mortem
A color-coded ring: the budget speaks in color before it speaks in numbers
Two-tap logging — record a coffee without leaving the home screen
Dark, single-accent system on Outfit, designed in Figma and built in React Native
galleryCaptions: Core screens — onboarding, the pace ring, and daily entry
Design system — color, type, spacing and components
Case-study board — the full narrative end to end

caseStudy.problem: Most budgeting apps tell you the truth too late. You overspend across three weeks, then a monthly report explains — calmly, in hindsight — exactly how. Pace had to move that signal to the front of the month: a single, glanceable answer to 'how much can I spend today without breaking the plan?' that updates the moment you spend.

process.heading: Reframing budget as pace
process.body: I started by throwing out the report. A monthly total is a verdict; what people actually need is a speedometer. So the core object became a daily allowance — the remaining budget divided across the days left in the cycle — that recalculates after every transaction. One number, scoped to today, that quietly absorbs the math behind it.

process.heading: Making the number speak in color
process.body: A figure alone still asks the user to interpret it. The pace ring carries a second, faster channel: it shifts color with your standing — ahead, on track, neutral, or over. You read the color before you read the digits, so the state lands in a glance even from a locked-screen widget. 'Tempo, renkle konuşur' — pace speaks in color — became the design principle.

process.heading: Designing entry to disappear
process.body: A budget app only works if logging is effortless, so the entry field lives on the home screen, right under the ring. Recent and frequent spends surface as one-tap chips — Kahve, Market, Ulaşım — so the common case is two taps and done. The faster logging gets, the more honest the number stays.

decisions.title: One number, scoped to today
decisions.rationale: Showing month-to-date totals, category pies, and projections at once is the default — and it's paralysis. I collapsed all of it into a single daily figure on the home screen and pushed the breakdowns one layer down. The home screen answers one question; everything else is on request.

decisions.title: Color before digits
decisions.rationale: Numbers require reading; color requires nothing. Binding the ring's color to budget state means the most important signal — am I okay? — arrives pre-attentively. The exact figure is there for when you want it, but the verdict doesn't wait for it.

decisions.title: Dark by default, one accent
decisions.rationale: A finance app gets opened in quick, glance-and-go moments. A dark surface keeps the ring and its color the brightest thing on screen, and holding to a single blue accent means the semantic colors (warn, over, danger) never have to compete with decorative ones.

outcome.narrative: Pace ships as a focused iOS app where the home screen does one job well: it tells you, right now, how much room today has — and colors that answer so you feel it before you read it. The design system (Outfit, a dark neutral ground, one blue accent plus a tight set of semantic colors) carries from the marketing board straight into the React Native build, so what was designed is what shipped.

learnings: Reframing the core metric — pace instead of report — changed more about the product than any screen-level decision did. The right object makes the rest of the UI obvious.
Color as a primary channel (not decoration) is the cheapest way to make a number feel instant. It does the work before the user spends attention.
Designing and building it myself meant the system survived contact with the runtime — the color tokens and spacing scale moved into React Native intact, with nothing lost in handoff.

walkthrough.title: Walkthrough
walkthrough.intro: A closer look at the home screen — the surface a user sees dozens of times a day. Each marker explains one decision behind making the budget readable in a single glance.
walkthrough.alt: Pace — home screen with the daily pace ring and quick entry

hotspots.title: One number, scoped to today
hotspots.body: The center of the ring isn't your monthly total or what you've spent — it's what you can still spend today and stay on plan. The remaining budget, divided by days left, recalculated after every entry. All the math is absorbed so the user reads one figure, not a spreadsheet.

hotspots.title: The ring speaks in color
hotspots.body: The arc's color, not just its length, encodes your standing — ahead, on track, neutral, over. It's the fastest channel on the screen: you register the color before you parse the digits, which is exactly the point on a surface you glance at, not study.

hotspots.title: Plain-language status
hotspots.body: '₺ / gün · YOLUNDA' (on track) names the state in words under the number. Color tells you instantly, the label confirms it unambiguously — belt and suspenders for the one judgment that matters, with no jargon between the user and the answer.

hotspots.title: Entry lives on the home screen
hotspots.body: 'Bugün ne harcadın?' — what did you spend today — sits directly under the ring. Logging isn't buried behind a tab or a plus-menu; it's the second thing on the primary screen, because a budget that's annoying to update stops being true.

hotspots.title: Frequent spends as one-tap chips
hotspots.body: Kahve ₺45, Market ₺320, Ulaşım ₺30 — recent and recurring amounts surface as chips, so the common case is a single tap. The faster the honest path, the less the number drifts from reality.

hotspots.title: The only loud button on screen
hotspots.body: The blue add button is the single saturated element in the lower half — the one action the screen actively invites. Everything else recedes into the dark ground so the primary verb, 'log a spend,' is never ambiguous.

items.title: Viola Resort Sapanca UI/UX Design
items.desc: An end-to-end design system and product interface for a boutique tiny-house resort with private heated pools in Sapanca — designed and built. A premium stay experience on a cinematic night palette, champagne-gold accents and glassmorphism surfaces.
highlights: End-to-end: design system, marketing site and booking flow — designed and built in Next.js
A 12-step warm-neutral ink scale with champagne-gold and olive accents, all from CSS variables
One token source, two themes — dark by default, a warm-cream light variant of the same system
Glassmorphism that stays WCAG-legible, and Safari-friendly GPU motion
galleryCaptions: Overview — the approach: a premium feel without losing accessibility
Color system — warm-neutral ink scale, champagne gold and olive
Typography — Manrope display over Inter body, fluid scale
Surface, shadow and motion — glass, elevation and easing tokens
Core screens — homepage hero and room detail
Gallery & social proof — nature, comfort, privacy
Responsive — the system carried onto mobile
Key decisions and the dark / light theme variants

caseStudy.problem: A boutique property's digital storefront usually gets trapped in one of two places: a generic booking-engine template, or a moody dark theme that looks premium but reads poorly. Viola Resort — fifteen private-pool tiny houses on Sapanca lake — needed a storefront that felt genuinely luxurious without ever sacrificing accessibility or the practical job of getting a guest to a reservation.

process.heading: A warm, cinematic ground
process.body: The palette started from the logo, not from a theme preset. I built a 12-step warm-neutral ink scale and a champagne-gold plus olive/sage accent family, all flowing from a single source of CSS variables. A dark surface keeps the night photography and the gold accent the brightest things on screen — the mood the property actually sells.

process.heading: Legibility as a hard constraint
process.body: 'Premium dark' fails the moment text drops below contrast. Body and small text sit on gold-400, which holds AA, while the lighter champagne tone is reserved for large decorative headings only. Glassmorphism panels use a sufficiently dark backing and strong blur so they stay readable in every condition — and gracefully raise their opacity where backdrop-filter isn't supported.

process.heading: Design that survived the build
process.body: I designed and built it, so the system had to survive contact with the runtime. Type scale, a 4pt spacing rhythm, radius, shadow and motion easings all moved into the Next.js build as tokens — the palettes on the case-study board are read from the same CSS variables the live site uses. What was designed is literally what shipped.

decisions.title: Two-tier gold
decisions.rationale: The champagne display tone is beautiful but too light for body copy. I split it: gold-400 for anything text-sized (AA, 4.5:1 holds), and the lighter signature tone strictly for large decorative headings. The brand color and the contrast requirement stop fighting each other.

decisions.title: Legible glass
decisions.rationale: Glassmorphism usually trades readability for style. These panels sit on a dark backing at 0.55–0.72 alpha with 24–32px blur, and opacity auto-increases where backdrop-filter isn't available — so the effect degrades to a solid, readable surface instead of unreadable haze.

decisions.title: Safari-friendly motion
decisions.rationale: The ambient orb drift originally animated scale on blurred layers, which forced a re-raster every frame and janked badly in Safari. Switching to translate3d only kept it on pure GPU compositing — the same motion, none of the jank.

decisions.title: One source, two themes
decisions.rationale: Dark is the default, but light isn't a separate design — it's a warm-cream variant of the exact same token set. Every color is read from CSS variables, so the two themes can never drift apart and adding the light mode cost almost nothing.

outcome.narrative: Viola Resort ships as a live, dark-luxury site that reads as calm, warm and premium without ever slipping below accessibility. The design system — a warm ink scale, champagne-gold and olive accents, glass surfaces and a 4pt rhythm — carries from the Figma board straight into the Next.js build, with dark and light as two faces of one token source. Quiet luxury, legible interface.

learnings: 'Premium' and 'accessible' are usually framed as a trade-off — but the constraints (two-tier gold, dark-backed glass) are exactly what gave the system its identity. Restraint read as luxury.
Sourcing every color from CSS variables meant the light theme, the case-study board and the live product could never disagree. One source of truth pays off the moment you need a second theme.
Building what I designed exposed the details a static mockup hides — the Safari re-raster, the backdrop-filter fallback. The system got tougher because it had to run, not just render.

walkthrough.title: Walkthrough
walkthrough.intro: A closer look at the homepage hero — the first surface a guest sees. Each marker explains one decision behind making a dark, cinematic screen feel both premium and effortless to act on.
walkthrough.alt: Viola Resort — homepage hero with an aerial night view of the resort

hotspots.title: Booking is always one tap away
hotspots.body: 'Check Availability' sits pinned top-right in a light pill — the single brightest control in the header. However far a guest scrolls into browsing mode, the primary action stays visible and unambiguous.

hotspots.title: Gold reserved for one phrase
hotspots.body: The headline is near-white except for 'heart of nature' in the champagne tone. Applying the signature gold to a decorative display line — never to body text — is what keeps it both on-brand and above the contrast line.

hotspots.title: The photograph carries the mood
hotspots.body: An aerial dusk shot of the lit tiny houses does the selling that copy can't. The dark UI ground exists to make this image — and the warm lights inside it — the loudest thing on the screen.

hotspots.title: Two CTAs, clear hierarchy
hotspots.body: 'Check Availability' is a solid pill; 'Discover Rooms' is a quiet outline. Guests ready to book and guests still browsing each get a path, but only one of them looks like the primary verb.

hotspots.title: Theme and language, out of the way
hotspots.body: The light/dark toggle and the TR/EN switch live as small marks at the top-right — present for the guest who needs them, weightless for the one who doesn't. The default (dark, local language) is already right for most.

items.title: Eşref Karot Website UI/UX Design
items.desc: A single-page site for a core drilling and concrete cutting firm in Bodrum. A design system that merges Swiss typography with the language of an engineering document — colour, type, grid, components and motion — shipped live in Next.js.
highlights: A visual direction drawn from the firm's own technical drawings, not the category's generic SaaS language
Six-part design system document covering colour, type, grid, components and motion
One primary action: a single orange block per screen — place the call
Designed in Figma, shipped as a Next.js static export in TR/EN
galleryCaptions: Technical file — direction, ground, accent and geometry
Colour — concrete-paper ground, ink scale and one orange
Typography — Archivo narrates, IBM Plex Mono measures
Grid and measure — 1240px, 12 columns, hairline structure
Components — four buttons, one primary action
Principles — the four rules holding the system up

caseStudy.problem: A core drilling and concrete cutting firm in Bodrum had no site at all; work arrived by word of mouth. Every site in this category looks identical: stock construction photography, shadowed cards, "15+ years of experience" badges and a contact form nobody fills in. The real problem wasn't building a page — it was establishing trust without reaching for the industry's own inflated language.

process.heading: Finding the right reference
process.body: I looked at the firm's own work rather than at competitor sites: technical drawings, dimension marks, bore diameter tables. Core drilling is millimetric work, and that precision already has a visual language. I decided to build the site as a technical file rather than a marketing brochure — the only way to separate from every site in the category and stay honest at the same time.

process.heading: Writing the system before drawing it
process.body: Before touching screens I built the system as a document: a concrete-paper ground and ink scale, type split between the one that narrates (Archivo) and the one that measures (IBM Plex Mono), a 1240px twelve-column grid, 0px radius and hairline rules with no shadow. Because the decisions were settled in the rules rather than on the screens, the screens came out fast and consistent.

process.heading: Reducing to one action
process.body: For this client the conversion is unambiguous: the phone rings. No forms, no accounts, no quote wizard. I reserved the orange block for exactly one job across the whole system — the call. Everything else (WhatsApp, equipment, services) stays at hairline-drawn secondary weight, and on mobile the primary button dials directly.

decisions.title: Only real data
decisions.rationale: I left out the invented "15+ years" and "500+ projects" statistics — no number I couldn't verify made it onto the page. Instead I put the one verified technical fact I had, the Ø18–400mm bore range, at the centre of the page.
decisions.impact: Trust built on a measurement, not a boast

decisions.title: Lines, not boxes
decisions.rationale: No shadows, no rounding; structure is carried by hairline rules. It pulls the result toward the construction industry's own drafting language — it reads like a file rather than a generic grid of SaaS cards.

decisions.title: Orange is a mark, not decoration
decisions.rationale: The accent never fills a surface, never becomes a gradient, never glows. It marks the number to call, the section order and the dimension line — nothing else. It works because it's rare; everywhere at once, it would point at nothing.
decisions.impact: Where the eye goes when it sees accent is never in doubt

outcome.narrative: The site is live at esrefkarot.com in TR/EN — the technical-file board in Figma and the shipped Next.js build share the same rules. The result inverts what's normal in the construction category: trust built from measurement and typography instead of stock photos and badges. The firm has one callable number, and I have a system I won't be renegotiating on the next client.

learnings: Copying a category's visual clichés feels like the safe option but is the expensive one — looking like everyone else lands in the same place as not being seen at all.
The "only real data" rule started as a constraint and ended up the strongest thing on the page: one verifiable number persuades more than five invented ones.

Writing the design system as a document before the screens looks like overkill for a single page — but because every decision was settled in the rules, the page itself became almost an act of execution.

walkthrough.title: Walkthrough
walkthrough.intro: The component section of the system — where the rule for every touchable part of the site is defined. Each mark explains a decision underneath the surface.
walkthrough.alt: Eşref Karot design system — component section

hotspots.title: The only filled block on the page
hotspots.body: The solid orange button is reserved for exactly one job across the system: placing the call. This firm's conversion isn't a signup flow, it's a ringing phone. Locking the accent to a single action removes the question of where to press entirely.

hotspots.title: A third weight — not even a button
hotspots.body: "Hizmetlerimiz ↓" is neither solid nor outline — just underlined text and a down arrow. An action that scrolls within the same page has no business dressing up as a button; the arrow says the destination never leaves the page. Three weights, three levels of commitment.

hotspots.title: The rule doesn't bend on dark
hotspots.body: The outline button flipping to white on the contrast section is expected — the real decision is that orange stays orange here too. Shifting the accent when the ground changes would have been easy, but it would break the "orange = phone" equation. The panel changes; the contract doesn't.

hotspots.title: Section mark — position, not heading
hotspots.body: Number in orange, name in ink, remaining width filled with a hairline rule. This isn't a heading, it's a tab in a technical file: it tells you where in the document you are, not what to read.

hotspots.title: The one statistic, and it's true
hotspots.body: Ø18–400mm is the firm's single verified technical capacity — and the only number on the page. Leaving out "500+ projects" and "15+ years" was deliberate: a figure I can't verify quietly erodes trust instead of building it. One real measurement outweighs five invented badges.

hotspots.title: File rows, not cards
hotspots.body: Five services laid out as numbered rows instead of a grid of shadowed cards. A card grid scatters the eye and makes five options equally vague; a numbered row imposes a single reading order — like the contents list of a technical file.

lab.lede: A workshop of interactive experiments. Each piece is built in code — no static mockups — to explore a specific motion or interaction principle. Designed and engineered end-to-end as a place to play.

cursorSymphony.title: Cursor Symphony
cursorSymphony.blurb: A cursor that breathes — state-morphs over text, buttons, and media, with magnetic snapping driven by spring physics.

tokenForge.title: Token Forge
tokenForge.blurb: A live design-token playground. Drag sliders to tune accent hue, radius, and density — watch the system re-flow.

driftPhysics.title: Drift Physics
driftPhysics.blurb: Throw a card; it tumbles, settles, and snaps back. Real momentum, constrained drag, spring damping.

scrollCinema.title: Scroll Cinema
scrollCinema.blurb: A scroll-linked cinematic sequence. Text reveals, parallax depth layers, and color theme shifts mapped to scroll progress.

systemCreator.title: System Creator
systemCreator.blurb: A full design-system builder. Color, typography, radius, stroke — every choice paints a live UI kit you can copy out as CSS or JSON.

typeSymphony.title: Type Symphony
typeSymphony.blurb: A variable-font playground. Drag five axes — weight, width, slant, optical size, grade — or press play and watch the letters dance through an 8-second choreography.

cursorSymphony.lede: Move your pointer across the surfaces below. The cursor remembers what it's touching — text gets a caret, links open into a ring, media expands into a viewfinder, and magnetic anchors pull it home.
cursorSymphony.textBody: Long-form text invites a precise caret. Notice how the cursor tightens into a slim vertical line so you can still see exactly which letter you're grazing — like an editor caret, not a blob.
cursorSymphony.linkBody: The cursor opens into a 64px ring and labels its intent.
cursorSymphony.magnetsBody: Each pellet pulls the cursor toward its center.
cursorSymphony.footerNote: <0>useMotionValue</0> for raw pointer coords, <0>useSpring</0> for two-layer smoothing, <0>useTransform</0> for ambient hue mapping, and a small state machine driving variant-style animation transitions.

tokenForge.lede: A working design system, miniature. Drag the sliders to retune accent hue, radius, density, and weight — every component you see below is bound to those tokens and re-flows live.
tokenForge.body: Every token is wired. Push a value; watch the system breathe.
tokenForge.footerNote: A reactive token graph: five state values feed every visual property. <0>LayoutGroup</0> + <0>motion.div layout</0> animates dimension changes when radius and density shift, so the system reads as alive rather than jerky.

driftPhysics.lede: Grab a card and throw it. The drag transition tracks velocity, adds momentum, and lets cards skid against the walls before springing back to rest. Double-click to dismiss.
driftPhysics.footerNote: <0>drag</0> + <0>dragConstraints</0> against a container ref, custom <0>dragTransition</0> for inertia tuning, and <0>useTransform</0> mapping x velocity to rotation for a hand-thrown feel.

scrollCinema.lede: A scroll-linked cinematic sequence. Text reveals, parallax depth layers, and color theme shifts mapped to scroll progress.
scrollCinema.reducedLede: Reduced-motion mode is enabled — the cinematic sequence is disabled. Below is a static summary of what this experiment showcases.
scrollCinema.act1Body: Every interaction is a small commitment between system and user. Motion is how we deliver on it — confirming, guiding, rewarding.
scrollCinema.act2Body: Easing is gravity. A spring that overshoots feels alive; a linear curve feels like a robot. Choose the physics, choose the feeling.
scrollCinema.act3Body: Choreographed reveals scaffold attention. The right element arrives the moment it's asked for — never sooner.
scrollCinema.footerNote: A single <0>useScroll</0> source spring-smoothed, then <0>useTransform</0>ed into a dozen choreographed outputs: color theme, parallax depths, sequential reveals, and a final stamp. One scroll variable, an entire scene.

systemCreator.lede: A working design system, in full. Tune primary hue, font pairing, radius scale, and stroke width — the preview rebuilds itself live with a 10-step palette, type scale, and components ready to copy out.
systemCreator.typeBody: Body text carries the meaning. It should read effortlessly, breathe between lines, and let the system fade away.
systemCreator.cardBody: A modular dashboard with reusable patterns across surfaces.
systemCreator.footerNote: Every visual choice flows from a small graph of tokens: <0>primary hue</0> generates a 10-step palette, <0>type scale</0> drives every text size, <0>radius</0> and <0>stroke</0> shape every surface and outline. <0>LayoutGroup</0> animates the in-between as you re-tune.

typeSymphony.lede: Variable fonts let one font file behave like an entire family. Drag the five axes below to bend Roboto Flex through its full design space, or press play to watch the letters dance through a choreographed 8-second symphony.
typeSymphony.editAria: Type to change the sample text
typeSymphony.footerNote: Roboto Flex carries five active axes (<0>wght</0>, <0>wdth</0>, <0>slnt</0>, <0>opsz</0>, <0>GRAD</0>). Each slider writes directly into <0>font-variation-settings</0>. Press play and <0>animate()</0> from framer-motion drives a single 0→1 timeline that maps to a four-phase choreography across all axes simultaneously.
